mxfile.agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) draw.io/29.5.2 Chrome/142.0.7444.265 Electron/39.6.1 Safari/537.36

mxCell.value:
ADA Bartholomew: Now, when you are running through the woods, you don't have any shoes on. What is on the ground?

Lauren Deal: Leaves, sticks, briars, fallen tree branches.

ADA Bartholomew: And is it light out?

Lauren Deal: No.

ADA Bartholomew: Is it still dark?

Lauren Deal: It's completely dark.

ADA Bartholomew: So were you able to see where you were going when you were running?
421 is 10 miles away.
<font style="color: light-dark(rgb(0, 0, 0), rgb(0, 0, 0)); font-weight: normal;" face="Georgia">Never Crossed Route 1</font>

The fence is 
short 
and easy 
to get over.
No one even bothered to listen to what Lauren was testifying to at trial.

This is the only
house that is 
close and 
"beside" the 
road.
The issue is that she ran the other direction. She has stated the direction she ran in two police interviews and on the stand.

Lauren Deal: No. The only thing that had a little bit of light was there was a road to my left, and it was a highway. Pretty sure it was, like, 421 . I don't know. There was a highway. Except there was a fence that went all the way down and barbed wire on the top of it. But there were random, like, light posts on the highway.

ADA Bartholomew: When you were running through the woods, did you fall at all?

Lauren Deal: I fell into a briar bush, and I hit my knee, and I had, like, thorns from the, like, tops of my toes to the bottom of my heels on both feet, and both of my feet had been cut up really bad on like on my toes.

ADA Bartholomew: So you stated that there was a road that you could see. Did you ever try to get to that road?

Lauren Deal: I walked over to it just to see, like, if I thought I could climb the fence and go over the go over the barbed wire on top, and I stood by the fence and I had my shirt pulled down as far as I could, and I was waving my arms and I was purposely willing to put myself in more harm's way just so somebody could help me, and then after I realized nobody was stopping, there was a house on my right, and I went partially in their front yard, and I screamed for help, but nobody would come outside.

ADA Bartholomew: So what did you do at that point?

Lauren Deal: There is nothing you could do. You just walk. You just pray somebody sees you.

ADA Bartholomew: And did any cars eventually stop?

Lauren Deal: There was a side road on the left side, like a side road, and a car pulled up on it eventually, but I stayed in the woods because I didn't know if it was the boys or if it was Ben or who it was.

ADA Bartholomew:  And what happened to that car?


Lauren Deal: It was Joseph. He saw me from where I was standing, and he stopped and he begged me to get in the car. He promised me he would not hurt me. He promised me he would not take me back. He said that his girlfriend had dealt with something similar, and he was going to get me help, and he was going to make sure I was safe, and it took a little while of him coaxing me and him, like, telling me he has a phone and will call, you know, whoever.


ADA Bartholomew: And did you eventually allow him to help you?


Lauren Deal: Yes.
Homes
Property Lines
Mostly Pine Needles
Mostly pine needles that lead to an open grass airfield.

The grass airfield that lies directly in the path of where she said she ran. 
She said she didnt notice it.
Lauren Deal: Leaves, sticks, briars, fallen tree branches.

Where was this chase taking place? the woods in this area - 
especially the direction she claimed to have ran has 
underbrush cut out and sparse trees. The area she "ran" 
through is only 130ft in width.
It wasn't dark. Comparing her statements against video and science her statements are losing everytime.

Lauren Deal was 5 feet 3 inches and weighed 180 pounds. This corresponds to a Body Mass Index (BMI) of approximately 31.9. According to standard BMI classifications for adults, this falls into the 'Obese' category (BMI 30 or higher), specifically Class I obesity (BMI 30–34.9).

The current narrative suggests that Deal outran two soldiers who run more than five miles a day. However, physiologically, an individual in her condition—compounded by smoking status—would typically be capable of running only a short distance (e.g., 100 yards) before experiencing exhaustion, dizziness, or nausea.  As intoxicated as she was, this number
would be cut in half.
 

Furthermore, Deal was stumbling and bouncing off chairs before even leaving the bar. The effects of alcohol combined with obesity would significantly hinder physical performance:
 

Balance and coordination: Studies show that even at 0.06–0.10% BAC, postural control deteriorates significantly. At higher levels (0.13–0.15%+), there is a major loss of balance and gross motor impairment.
 

Reaction time and proprioception: This makes sustaining a jogging motion extremely difficult; legs feel heavy or uncoordinated, and the body struggles to stay upright.
 

Endurance and energy: Acute intoxication reduces aerobic capacity by impairing glucose release from the liver and increasing lactate levels, leading to quicker fatigue.
 

Cardiovascular and respiratory effects: Combined with smoking (which already reduces lung function), breathing would likely become labored almost immediately.

Given these factors, the claim that she sustained a run over a significant distance is inconsistent with her reported condition and physical abilities.

<font style="font-size: 16px;">There is nothing about this accusation—and the story that accompanies it—that inspires the least bit of belief. We are asked to set aside common sense, the laws of logic, biomechanics, and even basic physics to pretend that any of Lauren Deal's allegations could be true.</font>
Where are the briar bushes?

When a narrative demands darkness and precise vision, concealment and recognition, fear and certainty to coexist in an illuminated open field, or the woods, no one is sure which one they are going with - it describes manufactured reality — not events.

ADA Bartholomew: At that time, were you sure whether or not you could trust them?

Lauren Deal: No. No, I felt very alone in that moment, and I didn't know if they were in on it. I didn't know if they knew, and I was not going to stick around to find out.

ADA Bartholomew: So they are calling your name. Did you ever go to where they were?

Lauren Deal: No, I kept running, and I could hear one on either side of me, and at one point, one of them said, we're in the military. We should be able to find her. And every time I heard them get close, I would stop and I would hide behind a tree, and then finally after I mean, I don't know how long it was because I didn't have my phone and I didn't have a sense of, like, time, but after awhile, they finally stopped chasing me.

At a lunar illumination level of approximately 70%, the moon provides significant ambient light during nighttime. This corresponds to the waxing or waning gibbous phase, when most of the moon’s surface reflects sunlight toward Earth.

In a non-urban outdoor environment—such as a rural area or open field—this level allows for clear visual orientation. A person with normal vision can discern large objects (trees, vehicles, people) at distances exceeding 50 feet, detect ground variations, and navigate without a flashlight.

Moonlight creates soft silvery contrast, reducing deep shadows. Facial recognition is possible at close range (~10–15 feet), whereas body silhouettes, posture, movement, and high-contrast objects (e.g., light clothing) remain distinguishable at greater distances.

Complete darkness is overstated here: this illumination supports object detection, muted colors, and basic navigation in open terrain. That is the irrefutable scientific fact of the matter. What follows is an examination of the information collected during the “investigation” from a couple of key witnesses, and the illogical contradictions that arise when their statements are compared with each other and with the known conditions of that night.

What are we supposed to believe? That it was pitch dark and DeMarco still saw her running through a field when she couldn’t see her hand in front of her face? Or her story of hiding in the woods but flagging down a vehicle that she could recognize from a distance? The lights from the vehicle didn’t blind her when she looked, and she could recognize DeMarco. She had apparently been running through complete darkness, so the lights should impact her eyes. Or that she could see his face from her location in the woods right after just saying it was pitch black. The fact that the two stories are completely different is bad enough; they can see in complete darkness. When they need to, it is a cause for concern.

The highway she referenced (421) is ~10 miles away. An open airfield offers no concealment or reason for

extended disorientation under moonlight. This extremely overweight woman outran two physically fit soldiers in pitch black

heading in the opposite direction of either of the roads she claimed she may have been found on.

These are just holes in a story. These are completely missing sections. There was no follow-up to reference in the record.
